sanskrit:
बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते।
तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम्॥
transliteration:
buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte
tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam
literal_meaning: One endowed with steady wisdom (buddhi-yuktaḥ) abandons in this very life both good and evil actions (ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte). Therefore engage yourself for the sake of yoga, for yoga is skill in action.
contextual_meaning: Krishna tells Arjuna that when the mind is anchored in steady awareness (buddhi-yukta), the psychological charge of both praise and blame, success and failure, is neutralized. The person is no longer driven by the compulsive need to repeat pleasant karma or avoid unpleasant karma; action becomes a spontaneous, skilful expression of present-moment intelligence.
psychological_state: Post-ambivalent clarity—neither chasing rewards nor avoiding punishments; the mind shifts from moral bookkeeping to engaged presence.
trigger_scenario: You just received a critical performance review at work. Instead of spiraling into shame or defensiveness, you take a breath, notice the surge of adrenaline, and choose to respond with curiosity and skill rather than react from old scripts of proving or hiding.
anchor_text: Act with steady mind, free from chasing good or fearing bad outcomes.
